days.title: At the shop: основни думи

grammar.title: Numbers 11–19
grammar.explanation: Teens in Bulgarian are formed with the digit + '-надесет' (often shortened to '-найсет' in speech): единадесет (11), дванадесет (12), тринадесет (13), четиринадесет (14), петнадесет (15), шестнадесет (16), седемнадесет (17), осемнадесет (18), деветнадесет (19). The stress falls on '-на-'. These are used for counting items in a shop and saying simple quantities. Bulgarian has no noun cases, so the noun after a number simply takes its plural form: дванадесет ябълки (twelve apples).

examples.english: I have eleven leva.
I am buying twelve apples.
There are fifteen customers in the shop.

reading.title: В магазина
reading.text: Продавачът е любезен. Клиентът купува хляб и плодове. Той има дванадесет лева.
reading.translation: The shop assistant is kind. The customer is buying bread and fruit. He has twelve leva.

days.speakingPrompt: Imagine you walk into a small shop. Greet the seller and say what you want to buy, then say how many leva you have (use a number between 11 and 19). Practise saying it aloud in Bulgarian.

days.title: Tens: 20, 30 … 100

grammar.title: Tens 20–100
grammar.explanation: The round tens are: двадесет (20), тридесет (30), четиридесет (40), петдесет (50), шестдесет (60), седемдесет (70), осемдесет (80), деветдесет (90), сто (100). To form numbers in between, join the ten and the unit with 'и' (and): двадесет и едно (21), тридесет и пет (35), осемдесет и осем (88). The noun stays in the plural and never changes its ending, because Bulgarian has no cases.

examples.english: The price is twenty leva.
I have thirty-five leva.
The coat costs one hundred leva.

reading.title: Цената
reading.text: Този магазин е евтин. Цената е двадесет лева. Плащам в брой на касата.
reading.translation: This shop is cheap. The price is twenty leva. I pay in cash at the checkout.

days.speakingPrompt: Say three prices aloud in Bulgarian using round tens (e.g. 30, 50, 100 leva), then say whether each one is cheap (евтино) or expensive (скъпо) for you.

days.title: Колко струва? Asking the price

vocab.exampleEn: How much is it in total?

grammar.title: Колко струва? — asking how much
grammar.explanation: To ask a price use 'Колко струва …?' (How much does … cost?) for one item, and 'Колко струват …?' for several items, because the verb 'струвам' agrees with the subject (струва = it costs, струват = they cost). The answer is 'Струва + number + лева': Струва двадесет лева. For a running total ask 'Колко струва общо?' (How much altogether?). 'Колко' can also count things: 'Колко ябълки?' (How many apples?).

examples.english: How much does this coat cost?
How much do these shoes cost?
In total it costs forty leva.

reading.title: Колко струва?
reading.text: — Колко струва това палто? — Струва четиридесет лева. — Не е скъпо, купувам го.
reading.translation: — How much does this coat cost? — It costs forty leva. — That is not expensive, I'll buy it.

days.speakingPrompt: Role-play asking the price of three different things in a shop. For each, ask 'Колко струва …?' and give yourself an answer with a number between 11 and 100 leva.

days.title: The lev and the stotinka

grammar.title: Лев и стотинка — using the currency
grammar.explanation: One lev (1 лев) divides into 100 stotinki (стотинки). After the number 'one' the singular is used: един лев, една стотинка. After any number from two upwards use the plural: два лева, пет лева, петдесет стотинки. Note the special masculine counting form 'лева' used after numbers. To give a mixed price, join the parts with 'и': пет лева и петдесет стотинки (5.50). The currency word comes after the number, never before it.

examples.russian: Това струва три лева и двадесет стотинки.
examples.english: This costs three leva and twenty stotinki.

examples.russian: Имам само една банкнота от двадесет лева.
examples.english: I only have one twenty-lev banknote.
The shop assistant gives change.

reading.title: Рестото
reading.text: Хлябът струва два лева и петдесет стотинки. Давам банкнота от пет лева. Продавачът дава ресто.
reading.translation: The bread costs two leva and fifty stotinki. I give a five-lev banknote. The shop assistant gives change.

days.speakingPrompt: Practise saying five mixed prices aloud, for example '3 лева и 40 стотинки'. Then say which banknote or coin you would use to pay.

days.title: Clothes & sizes

vocab.exampleEn: I want to try on the shirt.

grammar.title: Numbers with clothes: agreement & plural nouns
grammar.explanation: When you count things in a shop, the noun goes into the plural and keeps that one form for every number: две ризи, петнадесет ризи, сто ризи. Bulgarian has no cases, so the ending never changes with the number. Adjectives agree with the noun's gender and number: бяла риза (white shirt, f), черен панталон (black trousers, m), червени обувки (red shoes, pl). Remember 'да' + present tense to express wanting: 'Искам да пробвам роклята' (I want to try on the dress).

examples.english: I want to try on these shoes.
How much do the two shirts cost?
What size do you wear?

reading.title: В магазина за дрехи
reading.text: — Искам да пробвам тази риза. Какъв размер е? — Това е голям размер. — Колко струва? — Двадесет лева.
reading.translation: — I want to try on this shirt. What size is it? — This is a large size. — How much does it cost? — Twenty leva.

days.speakingPrompt: Describe three clothing items you would like to buy. For each, name the item, give a colour, say the size you want, and ask the price in Bulgarian.

days.title: Putting it together: a shopping trip

vocab.exampleEn: I want to buy a shirt.
The market is next to the shop.

grammar.title: Counting and totals in a real dialogue
grammar.explanation: Now we combine everything: numbers 11–100, 'колко струва/струват', and the lev. Ask for items with 'Искам …' or 'Търся …', ask the price with 'Колко струва?', and the total with 'Колко струва общо?'. The seller answers with a number plus лева/стотинки. To add an item use 'още' (more): 'Искам още един панталон' (I want one more pair of trousers). All nouns stay in the plural after numbers with no case change.

examples.english: I am looking for a shirt and trousers.

examples.russian: Има намаление: ризата струва петнадесет лева.
examples.english: There is a discount: the shirt costs fifteen leva.
In total it costs sixty leva.

reading.title: На пазара
reading.text: — Търся риза и обувки. — Ризата струва петнадесет лева, обувките — четиридесет. — Общо петдесет и пет лева. Дайте ми една торба.
reading.translation: — I'm looking for a shirt and shoes. — The shirt costs fifteen leva, the shoes forty. — Fifty-five leva in total. Give me a bag.

days.speakingPrompt: Act out a full shopping trip: greet the seller, say you are looking for two items, ask the price of each, ask for the total ('Колко струва общо?'), and ask for a bag. Use numbers between 11 and 100.

days.title: Review: Shopping & money

grammar.title: Week 11 recap: numbers, prices and the lev
grammar.explanation: This week you learned numbers 11–100: teens with '-надесет' (единадесет, петнадесет), tens (двадесет, тридесет … сто), and combined numbers joined with 'и' (тридесет и пет). You asked prices with 'Колко струва?' (one item) and 'Колко струват?' (several), and totals with 'Колко струва общо?'. You used the currency: лев / лева and стотинка / стотинки, with the singular after 'one' (един лев, една стотинка) and the plural after every other number. Remember: Bulgarian has no noun cases, the noun stays in the plural after numbers, and adjectives agree in gender and number (бяла риза, черен панталон, червени обувки).

examples.english: How much does this dress cost?

examples.russian: Струва осемдесет лева и петдесет стотинки.
examples.english: It costs eighty leva and fifty stotinki.
In total I have ninety leva.

reading.title: На касата
reading.text: — Добър ден! Търся рокля. — Тази струва четиридесет лева. Има намаление. — Добре, купувам я. Колко струва общо? — Четиридесет лева. Ето вашето ресто и една торба.
reading.translation: — Good day! I'm looking for a dress. — This one costs forty leva. There's a discount. — Good, I'll buy it. How much is it in total? — Forty leva. Here is your change and a bag.

days.speakingPrompt: Without notes, role-play both parts of a shopping conversation: ask for a clothing item, ask its price and the total, pay with leva and stotinki, and receive your change. Use at least three numbers between 11 and 100.
